coredata.dc:title: Multi-component determination based on high quantum yield “on-off-on” carbon quantum dots sensor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiang
Wu, Jun
Zhu, Xiashi
coredata.dc:description: The nitrogen(N)-sulfur(S)-sodium(Na(I)) co-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method, which exhibited a remarkably high fluorescence quantum yield (24.58%) and exceptional optical properties. The fluorescence “on-off-on” sensor was constructed. The fluorescence of CQDs was rapidly quenched with Fe(III) and the fluorescence recovered by ascorbic acid (Asc) partially and arginine (Arg)/histidine (His) completely. The CQDs fluorescence sensor demonstrated rapid response, exceptional sensitivity, excellent stability, remarkable selectivity, and robust anti-interference performance, which was feasible to simultaneously determine the concentrations of multiple analytes in the sample with satisfactory recovery rates. The “on-off-on” fluorescence mechanism of CQDs was investigated, revealing the significant potential of carbon nano-functionalized materials in the field of drug detection through fluorescence sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence “on-off-on” sensor
Fe(III)
Ascorbic acid
Arginine
Histidine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The process of actual sample preparation
The analytical performance of Fe(III) determination
Evaluation of the Fe(III) selectivity and anti-interference experiment
The analytical performance of Asc/Arg/His determination
Evaluation of Asc/Arg/His selectivity and anti-interference experiment
Determination of Fe(III), Asc, Arg and His in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The morphology and elemental functional group characterization of CQDs. (a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CQDs, with the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image and particle size distribution of CQDs shown in the top/bottom right insets, respectively; (b) The infrared (FT-IR) spectra of Cys (yellow curve) and CQDs (red curve); (c) Full XPS spectra of CQDs (inset shown different elemental contents); (d) The C1s and N1s high-resolution XPS spectra of CQDs; (e) The O1s and S2p high-resolution XPS spectra of CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The UV and fluorescence properties of CQDs (a) The UV absorption (blue curve), excitation (red dashed curve) and emission (red solid curve) spectra of CQDs, with the color of CQDs under 365 nm UV light shown in the inset; (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (300–400 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The chiral properties of CQDs. (a) The CD spectra of raw material L/D-Cys; (b) The CD spectra of the product L/D-CQDs.

Schematic diagram of CQDs fluorescence “on-off-on”. (a-c) Schematic diagram of fluorescence “on(CQDs)-off(CQDs-Fe(III))–on(CQDs-Fe(III)- Asc/Arg/His)” of CQDs, and the insets were the color variation under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp.

CQDs fluorescence “on-off-on” spectra and linear relationship. (a) Fluorescence intensity changes of CQDs with varying concentrations of Fe(III) (0–18.0 μM). The upper right inset was the linear relationship between ΔFoff and cFe(III), while the lower inset was the color alteration upon 365 nm UV light irradiation.; (ΔFoff = F0-F; F0 was the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs; F was the fluorescence intensity after the addition of Fe(III) to CQDs); (b-d) The fluorescence intensity of CQDs-Fe(III) changes with the addition of different concentrations of Asc (0–25.0 μM)/Arg (0–150.0 μM)/His (0–150.0 μM) to CQDs-Fe(III).The upper right insets illustrated the linear relationship between ΔFon and cAsc/Arg/His, respectively, while the lower insets demonstrated the color changes under 365 nm UV light irradiation; (ΔFon = F-F0; F0 was the fluorescence intensity after the addition of Fe(III) to the CQDs solution; F was the fluorescence intensity after the addition of Asc/Arg/His to the CQDs-Fe(III)).

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CQDs. (a) Stern-Volmer equation for CQDs-Fe(III) at different temperature (293 K,313 K,333 K); (b) Absorption spectra of CQDs (red curve), Fe(III) (blue curve) and CQDs-Fe(III) (yellow curve). The inset shown the absorption spectra of Fe(III) (blue curve), fluorescence excitation (red dashed curve) and emission (red solid curve) spectra of CQDs; (c) Zeta potentials of CQDs, CQDs-Fe(III), CQDs-Fe(III)-Asc, CQDs-Fe(III)-Arg, CQDs-Fe(III)-His. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The fluorescence recovery mechanism of CQDs. (a-c) Absorption spectra of Fe(III) (red curve), Asc/Arg/His (blue curve), and Fe(III)-Asc/Arg/His (yellow curve), the insets showed the corresponding local magnification; (d-f) CD spectra of Asc/Arg/His (red curve), Asc/Arg/His-Fe(III) (blue curve); (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: LOD was the limit of detection, s was the standard deviation measured by 10 blank probe samples, k was the slope of the calibration plot. The Fe(Ⅲ)/Asc/Arg/His low concentration standard curve presented in Fig. S4.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of Fe(III) content in different water samples.
Detection of Asc/Arg/His content in different samples.

head.ce:title: Multi-component determination based on high quantum yield “on-off-on” carbon quantum dots sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, College of Guangling, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, College of Guangling, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nitrogen(N)-sulfur(S)-sodium(Na(I)) co-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method, which exhibited a remarkably high fluorescence quantum yield (24.58%) and exceptional optical properties. The fluorescence “on-off-on” sensor was constructed. The fluorescence of CQDs was rapidly quenched with Fe(III) and the fluorescence recovered by ascorbic acid (Asc) partially and arginine (Arg)/histidine (His) completely. The CQDs fluorescence sensor demonstrated rapid response, exceptional sensitivity, excellent stability, remarkable selectivity, and robust anti-interference performance, which was feasible to simultaneously determine the concentrations of multiple analytes in the sample with satisfactory recovery rates. The “on-off-on” fluorescence mechanism of CQDs was investigated, revealing the significant potential of carbon nano-functionalized materials in the field of drug detection through fluorescence sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered significant attention from researchers [1] due to its water solubility [2], low toxicity [3], fluorescence properties [4], abundance and cheap precursors. These inherent advantages have led to their widespread utilization in diverse fields such as fluorescence probe [5], bioimaging [6], antibacterial agents [7], chiral recognition systems [8], modulation of enzyme activity [9] and sensors [10]. The research efforts were primarily focused on functionalized CQDs, with heteroatom doping being the simplest and most effective method. Among these methods, the doping of elements such as nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), phosphorus (P), chlorine (Cl) into CQDs could effectively modify their surface electron layer structure and create additional active sites, thereby significantly enhancing their optical properties [11]. Notably, the incorporation of sodium ions as metal dopants in CQDs leads to a pronounced emission of intense blue fluorescence, enabling facile visual observation [12].

Fluorescence sensor offer the advantages of rapid response, high sensitivity, low detection limit, excellent selectivity, and obviate the need for expensive instrumentation [13,14]. In recent years, CQDs fluorescence sensors have been successfully developed and applied in food and drug analysis [15]. Elshenawy et al. developed a facile, rapid, and selective fluorescence sensor for the determination of tilmicosin based on novel nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon quantum dots. This method exhibited high energy efficiency and environmentally friendly synthesis [16]. Gao et al. synthesized nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon quantum dots to construct a fluorescence “on-off-on” sensor for the rapid detection of Hg2+, MnO4 −, and halogen ions. This approach offered simplicity in operation, cost-effectiveness, and visual recognition [17]. The aforementioned studies have demonstrated the feasibility of CQDs as fluorescence sensors and their promising prospects in areas such as food and drug detection. However, the attainment of multi-component content determination necessitates further enhancements.

In this work, the nitrogen(N)-sulfur(S)-sodium(Na(I)) co-doped CQDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. A comprehensive characterization of the CQDs was conducted to establish fluorescence “on-off-on” sensor for the detection of Fe(III), ascorbic acid (Asc), arginine (Arg), histidine (His). The fluorescence “on-off-on” mechanism of CQDs has been thoroughly investigated. The sensor exhibited notable advantages in terms of rapid response, high stability, and exceptional sensitivity. Moreover, satisfactory recoveries were achieved for Fe(III), Asc, Arg, and His in real samples.

In this experiment, ascorbic acid, tartaric acid, and other amino acids were used in the common L configuration (glycine is not chiral), except for two configurations of L/D-cysteine (L/D-Cys), which served as a raw material for carbon quantum dots preparation. Ascorbic acid (Asc), arginine (Arg), histidine (His), glutamine (Gln), phenylalanine (Phe), alanine (Ala), leucine (Leu), aspartic acid (Asp), proline (Pro), isoleucine(Ile), tryptophan(Trp), glutamic acid(Glu), methionine(Met), glycine(Gly), tartaric acid(Tar), cysteines(Cye) citric acid(CA), urea, NaOH, FeCl3, FeSO4·7H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O , AlCl3, AgNO3, NaCl, KCl, BaCl2, ZnCl, CaCl2, MgSO4, H2SO4, quinine sulphate and regenerated cellulose dialysis bags with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Co. The buffer solution used was PBS buffer solution. Disposable syringes with a capacity of 5 mL were obtained from Frontier Medical Devices Co., while pinhole membrane filters with a pore size of 0.22 μm were purchased from Tianjin Experimental Equipment Co.

TEM images were obtained with a HT7800 transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). HRTEM images were obtained with a Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN (FEI, USA). XPS spectra were obtained with an ESCALAB 250Xi X photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). FT-IR spectra were obtained with a Tensor27 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). FL spectra were obtained on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–Vis spectra were obtained on a Thermo-1780 UV spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). CD spectra were obtained using a J-810 meta-dichroism spectrometer (JASCO, Japan). Zeta potentials were obtained on a ZEN3690 potential analyzer (Malvern, UK).

The CQDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method employing L/D-Cys as the sulfur dopant, CA as the carbon source, Urea as the nitrogen dopant, and NaOH as the sodium ion dopant. The L/D-Cys (1.0 g), CA (1.0 g), Urea (0.5 g), NaOH (0.09 g), and H2O (30.00 mL) were combined in a beaker and subjected to ultrasonic agitation for 10 min until complete dissolution was achieved. Subsequently, the homogenized solution was transferred into a 100 mL reactor and allowed to react at 120 °C for 16 h. The crude product was initially passed through a 0.22 μm pinhole syringe to eliminate macroscopic by-product particles, followed by filtration using a 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove unreacted raw materials and microscopic by-product particles. Finally, the resulting solution of CQDs was stored at 4 °C under light-free conditions (The concentration of treated CQDs dispersion was set to 1.0 mg·mL−1).

The QY was determined using the reference method with quinine sulphate as a standard reference [18].
Q Y = Y s ×
I U
I S
×
A S
A U
×
η u
η s
2

Ys (0.54) was the fluorescence quantum yield of quinine sulphate in 0.1 mol·L−1 sulfuric acid; I was the fluorescence integral area at the maximum excitation wavelength of 365 nm; A was the absorbance at 365 nm (A should be controlled to be less than 0.1 in order to reduce self-absorption effect). η was the refractive index of the solvent (ηu/ηs was approximately equal to 1). Subscripts “U” and “S” indicate CQDs and quinine sulphate, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The process of actual sample preparation
ce:section.ce:para: The tap water and river water samples were each collected in 5.0 mL volumes and subjected to triple filtration through a 0.22 μm pore size membrane filter before being set aside. The Vitamin C effervescent tablets, arginine ibuprofen tablets, and L-histidine nutritional granules were individually pulverized into fine powder. Subsequently, 0.1 g of each powder was dissolved in 30.00 mL of deionized water and subjected to ultrasonication for a duration of 10 min. Following this, the resulting mixtures were filtered three times through pinhole filters with a pore size of 0.22 μm before being set aside for further use.

The morphology of the prepared CQDs was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the CQDs exhibited a homogeneous distribution of spherical nanoparticles. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the CQDs (top right corner of Fig. 1(a)) clearly revealed well-defined lattice stripes with a precise lattice spacing of 0.30 nm, corresponding to the highly ordered graphitic carbon (002) crystal surface [19]. The size measurements of the particles in the TEM revealed that the average particle diameter in the CQDs was 3.76 ± 0.83 nm (Fig. 1(a) bottom right).

The FT-IR spectra of Cys (yellow curve) and CQDs (red curve) were depicted in Fig. 1(b). The broad absorption peak of CQDs at 3448 cm−1 could be attributed to the contraction vibration of O–H bonds [20,21]. The observed broadening of this spectral band, compared to the feedstock, may arise from the formation of hydrogen bonding interactions. The characteristic peaks at 3168 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 were ascribed to the contraction vibrations of N–H [22]. The characteristic peaks at 2992 cm−1, 2538 cm−1, and 1192 cm−1 were attributed to the contraction vibration of C–H, S-H, and C-S groups, respectively [23]. The characteristic peaks of S-H and C-S groups in the CQDs were less pronounced compared to those observed in the raw materials, potentially attributed to partial bond cleavage of S-H and C-S groups resulting from the high-temperature hydrothermal reaction. The characteristic peaks at 1616 cm−1, 1336 cm−1, 1292 cm−1, and 1047 cm−1 were contraction vibrations of C = O, COO–, C-N, and C-O groups [24]. The FT-IR analysis confirmed the successful synthesis of the CQDs.

The XPS full spectra of CQDs in Fig. 1(c) exhibited the presence of five elements, namely carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and sodium (Na). Notably, this elemental composition remained consistent with that of the raw material. The inset displayed the content of five elements, and the findings revealed successful doping of three elements, namely N, S, and Na. The high-resolution spectra of CQDs for C1s and N1s were depicted in Fig. 1(d). The characteristic peak at 284.8 eV can be attributed to the presence of C–C and C = C bonds [25], while those at 286.0 eV indicate the existence of C-N and C-O bonds [9]. Additionally, the peaks observed at 287.9 eV and 288.7 eV correspond to the presence of C = O and O-C = O bonds, respectively [26]. The characteristic peak observed at approximately 399.7 eV in the N1s spectra was attributed to pyrrole N and N = O, whereas the distinctive peak around 401.1 eV was assigned to pyridine N [27]. The high-resolution spectra of CQDs for O1s and S2p were presented in Fig. 1(e), exhibiting characteristic peaks at 531.4 eV and 532.9 eV, respectively, which can be ascribed to the presence of C-O and C = O [28]. The characteristic peaks observed at 163.4 eV and 164.6 eV in the high-resolution S2p spectra of CQDs were attributed to the S-H and C-S bonds originating from the feedstock cysteine [8]. The FT-IR and XPS results presented above confirm the successful doping of N, S, and Na elements, thereby establishing the presence of diverse elemental functional groups on the surfaces of CQDs.

The UV absorption spectra (blue curve), fluorescence excitation spectra (red dashed curve), and emission spectra (red solid curve) of CQDs were depicted in Fig. 2 (a). The UV absorption spectra of CQDs exhibited a prominent absorption peak at 241 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of C = C bonds [29]. Additionally, a distinctive peak at 330 nm was observed, corresponding to the n-π* transition involving C = O and C-N moieties [8]. The excitation peak of CQDs was observed at 373 nm, while the emission exhibited a maximum peak at 451 nm. The inset displayed the color of the CQDs solution when exposed to UV lamp irradiation at 365 nm, with the remarkable blue fluorescence originating from the latter portion of the absorption curve [30].

The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths were presented in Fig. 2(b). It could be observed that (1) the position of the emission peaks undergoes a red-shift as the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 400 nm, this phenomenon indicated the excitation-dependent nature of the CQDs, which may be attributed to variations in particle sizes and distributions of functional groups on their surface [31,32]; (2) the emission peaks initially increased and then decreased, reaching maximum intensity at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. (Subsequent experiments were carried out at 365 nm excitation wavelength).

The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs was determined to be 24.58% upon excitation at 365 nm, which exceeded that of the undoped/single-element doped CQDs as well as the CQDs previously reported in the literature (Table 1 ). The higher fluorescence quantum yield further confirmed the successful doping of Na, N and S elements, which effectively promoted the electron transfer rate on the surface of CQDs [27], thus providing CQDs with abundant surface sites and superior anti-interference properties [33].

The CD spectra of the raw material L/D-Cys and the product L/D-CQDs were depicted in Fig. 3 (a, b). The L/D-CQDs exhibited distinct characteristic peaks at 219 nm and 257 nm, but the CD intensity was significantly lower compared to that of the raw material at 200 nm. It was noteworthy that CQDs synthesized through high-temperature carbonization of chiral source (L/D-Cys) still exhibited some chiral characteristics.
The construction of a fluorescence sensor necessitates the possession of CQDs with exceptional optical properties and stability under experimental conditions.

The stability of CQDs was investigated in different conditions (cNaCl, Temperature, LED light irradiation time, and pH), as depicted in Fig. S1(a-d). The results demonstrated that a strong ionic strength stability, thermal stability, optical stability and pH stability (alkaline) of CQDs. The fluorescent groups in CQDs possess acidic/basic luminescent sites, and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs is influenced by solution acidity and alkalinity through protonation or deprotonation of amide and carboxyl groups with H+ and OH−, resulting in strong fluorescence intensity of CQDs in alkaline media [35,36]. Relevant experiments should therefore be conducted under alkaline conditions.

The schematic of fluorescence “ on–off-on” sensor for CQDs was shown in Fig. 4 (a-c). Curves 1 and 2 were the fluorescence spectra of CQDs and CQDs-Fe(III); Curve 3 was the fluorescence spectra of CQDs-Fe(III)-Asc/Arg/His respectively. The results as follows: (1) The fluorescence of CQDs could be quenched by Fe(III)(ΔFoff); (2) The fluorescence of CQDs could be partially recovered by Asc (ΔFoff﹥ΔFon); (3) The fluorescence of CQDs was completely recovered by Arg/His (ΔFoff = ΔFon). So, this fluorescence “on-off-on” sensor could be detected for multi-component (Fe(III), Asc, Arg and His).

The CQDs fluorescence “off” sensor (ΔFoff) were investigated at different reaction times (Fig. S2(a)), pH levels (Fig. S2(e)) and different concentrations of CQDs/Fe(III) (Fig. S2(i)/(j)). The optimal conditions for this fluorescence “off” sensor was a 3-minute incubation in a pH = 7.0 buffer solution, and it worsened at higher temperatures (decreased stability of Fe(III)-CQDs) [37,38]. So, room temperature was chosen as the experiment temperature (cCQDs = 20.0 μg·mL−1; cFe(Ⅲ) = 18.0 μM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analytical performance of Fe(III) determination
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity (F) of CQDs was decreased with increasing Fe(III) concentration (0–18.0 μM), as depicted in Fig. 5 (a). The inset in the upper right corner demonstrated a good linear relationship between the ΔFoff and cFe(III). Additionally, the lower inset displayed color changes under UV lamp irradiation at a wavelength of 365 nm. Notably, the blue luminescence gradually weakened with increasing Fe(III) concentration, which is consistent with the observed reduction of F.

The fluorescence “off” analytical performance of CQDs ΔFoff was presented in Table 2 . In comparison with the existing literature, this method exhibited notable advantages in terms of high sensitivity and low detection limit for Fe(III) detection (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the Fe(III) selectivity and anti-interference experiment
ce:section.ce:para: Selection of potential interfering substances (metal ions) to assess the method's selectivity and anti-interference capability. The fluorescence of CQDs was effectively quenched by Fe(III), Fe(II) and Cu(II) showed a slight quenching at the same concentration, other metal ions exhibited negligible impact. (Fig. S3(a)).
The F ratios (F/F0) for the addition of various metal ions (at a concentration five times that of cFe(III)) to CQDs-Fe(III) were depicted in Fig. S3(c), with F/F0 remaining essentially unaltered.

The above results demonstrated the excellent selectivity and anti-interference performance of this fluorescence sensor towards Fe(III), rendering it suitable for accurate determination of Fe(III) content in real samples.

The CQDs fluorescence “on” sensor (ΔFon) were investigated in reaction times (Fig. S2(b-d)) and pH (Fig. S2(f-h)). The optimal conditions for this fluorescence “on” sensor was determined to be a 3-minute incubation in a buffer solution at pH = 7, conducted at room temperature (cCQDs = 20.0 μg·mL−1; cFe(Ⅲ) = 18.0 μM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analytical performance of Asc/Arg/His determination
ce:section.ce:para: The F of CQDs was recovered to varying extents by the addition of different concentrations of Asc and Arg/His solutions to the CQDs-Fe(III) system, as depicted in Fig. 5(b-d). The top right inset depicted the linear relationship between ΔFon and cAsc/Arg/His. Meanwhile, the bottom inset illustrated the color alteration upon UV light irradiation at a wavelength of 365 nm. The blue luminescence exhibited a gradual increase in tandem with the ascending concentration of cAsc/Arg/His, aligning precisely with the concurrent alteration in F.

The fluorescence “ on ” performance of CQDs was analyzed as shown in Table 2. Additionally, Table S1 provided a comprehensive comparison between our present method for Asc/Arg/His detection and other relevant literature, highlighting its high sensitivity, low detection limit, and various advantages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of Asc/Arg/His selectivity and anti-interference experiment
ce:section.ce:para: Potential interfering substances (amino acids) were selected to assess the method's selectivity and anti-interference ability. As depicted in Fig. S3(b), the fluorescence of CQDs was partially/completely recovered by Asc and Arg/His, while no significant impact was observed with other amino acids.

The F ratios (F/F0) for the addition of other amino acids (amino acid concentration 5 times that of cAsc/Arg/His, respectively) in CQDs-Fe(III)-Asc/Arg/His were depicted in Fig. S3(d-f), while maintaining a constant F/F0.

The above results demonstrated that this fluorescence “on” sensor exhibited excellent selectivity and robust anti-interference performance towards Asc/Arg/His, rendering it suitable for the quantitative determination of Asc/Arg/His content in authentic samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe(III), Asc, Arg and His in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The present study aimed to determine the concentrations of Fe(III) (Tap water, River water) as well as Asc/Arg/His (Vitamin C Tablets/ Arginine Ibuprofen Tablets/ L-Histidine Nutritional Granules) in various samples. The detection performance of this sensor was evaluated using the standard adding method. The results of Fe(III) and Asc/Arg/His content detection were presented in Table 3 and Table 4 . The excellent recovery and low RSD demonstrated the applicability of the fluorescence “on-off-on” sensor developed in this study for real sample analysis.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CQDs was subsequently investigated. The type of fluorescence quenching was classified as static/dynamic quenching which was determined by calculating the quenching constant Ksv from Stern-Volmer equation [39]. The static quenching Ksv decreased with increasing temperature, while dynamic quenching exhibited an opposite trend [40]. The results were as follows: (1) There were good linear relationships between 1-F/F0 and cFe(III) in Fig. 6 (a); (2) The Ksv exhibited a negative correlation with temperature within the range of 293–333 K. The above analyses indicated that the fluorescence quenching was static. Thus, CQDs might polymerize with Fe(III) to produce weakly fluorescent or non-fluorescent ligand compounds.
The interaction mechanism between CQDs and Fe(III) was further investigated through UV absorption spectra analysis and zeta potential measurements.

(1) The absorption spectra of Fe(III) (blue curve) in the inset of Fig. 6(b) exhibited partial overlap with the fluorescence excitation spectra of CQDs (red dashed curve), indicating that the quenching of CQDs fluorescence by Fe(III) could be attributed to an internal filtration effect (IFE) [31,38,41].

(2) As depicted in Fig. 6(b), the Fe(III)-CQDs showed a significant increase in the intensity of the absorption peak at 330 nm and the disappearance of the absorption peak at 297 nm (Fe(III)), which may be attributed to the weakly fluorescent/non-fluorescent complex produced by static quenching [42-44].

(3) As shown in Fig. 6(c), the interaction between CQDs and Fe(III) were further investigated with the zeta potential. The zeta potential of CQDs was −13.2 mV, which was attributed to the presence of surface electron-absorbing groups (–COOH). Upon the addition of Fe(III) ions, the zeta potential increased to 4.0 mV, indicating that fluorescence quenching was also influenced by electrostatic adsorption [32]. The interaction between Fe(III) and the functional groups on the surface of CQDs induced a transition from the excited state to the ground state energy level, resulting in fluorescence quenching.

In summary, the fluorescence quenching process could be attributed to the formation of weakly fluorescent or non-fluorescent matrix complexes through IFE and electrostatic adsorption between multiple functional groups (carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl, etc.) on the surface of CQDs and Fe(III).
The fluorescence of CQDs could be partially and completely recovered by Asc and Arg/His, as illustrated in Fig. 4.
The absorption spectra were no significant changed in Asc/Arg/His-CQDs systems (Fig. S5(a-c)), It showed that there was no obvious interaction between CQDs and Asc/Arg/His.

The intensity of the Asc absorption peak at 270 nm was attenuated upon addition of Fe(III), the absorption peak of Fe(III) at 297 nm was vanished (Fig. 7 (a)) and the CD peak intensity of Asc at 252 nm significantly decreased after the addition of Fe(III) (Fig. 7(d)), which confirmed the reaction between Asc and Fe(III). The possible reaction mechanism may be that Asc molecule, containing highly reductive –OH and –CHO groups, efficiently converted Fe(III) to Fe(II) [45]. The zeta potential of CQDs-Fe(III) was also decreased from 4.0 mV to 1.6 mV after adding Asc (Fig. 6(c)), thus confirming the aforementioned speculation. As a result, Fe(III) were removed from the surface of CQDs, releasing the CQDs. Given that Fe(II) still exhibited certain quenching effect on CQDs (Fig. S3(a)), So, the fluorescence of CQDs was not completely recovered ΔFoff﹥ΔFon.

The characteristic peak at 297 nm in the Arg/His absorption curves disappeared with the addition of Fe(III), while new weak absorption peaks appeared at 374 nm/367 nm, respectively (Fig. 7(b)(c)), and the intensity of CD signals at 207 nm/213 nm were decreased for Arg/His (Fig. 7(e)(f)). The results indicated that Fe(III) was interacted with Arg/His. The reason of fluorescence complete recovery may be that (1) the amino acids exhibit a strong affinity towards metal ions and the N atoms preferentially coordinated with Fe(III) to form more stable compounds [32]; (2)This coordination results in the release of CQDs, which leads to a complete recovery of fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the zeta potential of CQDs-Fe(III) was significantly recovered to −12.2 mV/-11.8 mV after the addition of Arg/His, which was essentially consistent with that of CQDs (-13.2 mV), thus supporting the above hypothesis.

The mechanism of this fluorescence “on-off-on” sensor was schematically shown in Fig. 8 . (1) Fe(III) formed weakly fluorescent or non-fluorescent ground state compounds with CQDs, leading to static quenching. (2) The redox reaction between Asc and Fe(III) resulted in the reduction of Fe(III) to Fe(II), thereby inducing a partial recovery of CQDs fluorescence. (3) Arg/His generated stable complexes with Fe(III), which released CQDs and completely recovered the fluorescence of CQDs. (4) kAsc(Fluorescence partial recovery)＞kArg/His. (Fluorescence complete recovery) (Table 2), It might be that the mechanism of interaction between Asc and Arg/His with Fe(III) were different. More Arg/His may be needed to react with the same amount of Fe(III) than Asc. That is, more Arg/His was needed to recover the fluorescence. [46-48].

The present study focused on the synthesis of nitrogen, sulfur, and sodium co-doped CQDs for the development of a fluorescence “on-off-on” sensor. The detection of Fe(III), Asc, Arg, and His through a simple fluorescence “on-off-on” mechanism. The excellent optical properties of CQDs and the emission wavelength falling within the visible range enable convenient visualization of fluorescence changes under UV light irradiation, suggesting that CQDs hold promise for facilitating simpler and more convenient visual detection in subsequent studies. This work expanded the potential applications of carbon nanomaterials in fluorescence sensing technology for food and drug detection.
Xiang Li: Conceptualization. Jun Wu: Validation. Xiashi Zhu: Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375117) and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123662.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
